content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
How much does it really cost to rent your first apartmentâ€”
and what should you know before signing a lease?

slides.speakerNotes: Read Emma's apartment scenario aloud or have students read silently. Discussion: "What surprised you most about Emma's costs?" Ask students to estimate what they think renting costs in your local area.

objectives.description: Explain the key components of a lease agreement and what to look for before signing a legally binding rental contract.
Determine the true costs of renting, including rent, security deposits, utilities, fees, and hidden expenses beyond monthly rent.
Assess the advantages and disadvantages of renting compared to other housing options based on personal circumstances.
Recognize your rights and responsibilities as a tenant, including what landlords can and cannot legally do.

terms.definition: A legally binding contract between a landlord and tenant outlining rental terms, typically for 6-12 months.
terms.example: Rent amount, due dates, late fees, security deposit terms, policies, move-out notice requirements

terms.definition: Money paid upfront to cover potential damages beyond normal wear and tearâ€”typically refundable.
terms.example: Usually 1-2 months' rent; returned within 14-30 days after move-out (varies by state)

terms.definition: Insurance policy that protects a tenant's personal belongings and provides liability coverage.
terms.example: Landlord insurance only covers the buildingâ€”YOUR stuff isn't protected without renter's insurance

terms.definition: Services like electricity, water, gas, internet, and trash that may or may not be included in rent.

layoutData.title: 7 Key Components of Every Lease Agreement
paragraphs: A lease is a legally binding contract. Read every word before signingâ€”you're agreeing to follow these terms for the entire lease duration.
bulletPoints: Names & Addresses: All tenants listed, property address, landlord contact info
Rent Details: Amount, due date, payment methods, late fees, grace periods
Security Deposit: Amount, refund conditions, timeline, deduction policies
Lease Duration: Start/end dates, renewal options, early termination fees
Policies & Rules: Pets, guests, noise, smoking, alterations allowed
Entry Rights: When landlord can enter (usually 24-48 hour notice required)

content.headerTitle: Lease Red Flags to Watch For

leftPanel.title: Warning Signs in Lease Agreements
paragraphs: Not all leases are created equal. Some contain clauses that could cost you money or limit your rights. Be cautious if you see:
Vague language about when or how security deposits will be returned.
Unclear maintenance responsibilities that could leave you paying for repairs.
Excessive fees for basic services or common situations.

highlightBox.text: âš ď¸Ź Never sign a lease that requires you to waive legal rights or contains clauses that violate tenant protection laws.

leftPanel.title: What You'll Pay Before Move-In
paragraphs: Moving into a rental requires significant upfront investmentâ€”often 3-4 months of rent before you ever unpack a box.

layoutData.title: Beyond Just the Rent Payment
paragraphs: Your monthly rent is just the base cost. Your true monthly housing expense includes many additional items that can add $200-$500+ to your budget.

keyPoint.text: đź’ˇ Always ask "What's included in rent?" and budget 25-35% extra for utilities and fees.

leftPanel.title: Costs That Surprise New Renters
paragraphs: Even careful budgeters can be caught off guard by fees and costs that aren't obvious upfront.
Late payment fees are typically 5-10% of your rentâ€”a $1,200 rent could mean a $60-$120 late fee.
Annual rent increases of 3-5% are standard. A $1,200 apartment could be $1,260 next year.
Early termination fees if you need to break your lease are often 2-3 months' rent.

highlightBox.text: í´Ť Ask about ALL fees before signing. Get a complete list in writing.

leftColumn.title: âś“ Advantages
items: No property taxes or HOA fees

slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:
â€˘ ""The security deposit is the same as first month's rent."" â†’ They serve different purposes. First month's rent pays for housing; security deposit is held for potential damages and should be returned when you move out.
â€˘ ""Landlords can enter whenever they want."" â†’ Tenants have privacy rights. Landlords must provide advance notice (usually 24-48 hours) except in genuine emergencies like fire or flood.
â€˘ ""Renter's insurance only covers stolen items."" â†’ It also provides liability coverage if someone is injured in your apartment and protects against various damages (fire, water, etc.).

leftPanel.title: Your Legal Protections as a Renter
paragraphs: As a tenant, you have legal rights that protect you from unfair treatment. These vary by state but generally include:
Right to Habitable Housing: Your rental must be safe, clean, and in good repair with working utilities.
Right to Privacy: Landlords must give proper notice (usually 24-48 hours) before entering, except in emergencies.
Right to Security Deposit Protection: Laws regulate how deposits are held and returned.

highlightBox.text: âš–ď¸Ź If your landlord violates your rights, document everything and know how to file complaints with local housing authorities.

scenario.name: Emma's First Apartment
paragraphs: Emma recently graduated from college and moved to a new city for her first job. She found a one-bedroom apartment for $1,200/month in a convenient location near work.

layoutData.title: Being a Good Tenant = Protecting Yourself
paragraphs: Fulfilling your responsibilities as a tenant protects your deposit, your rental history, and your ability to rent in the future. Landlords share references.
bulletPoints: Pay Rent on Time: Late payments = fees + negative rental history
Maintain the Property: Keep it clean, don't cause damage beyond normal wear
Report Problems Promptly: Small issues become big (expensive) problems
Follow Lease Terms: Pets, guests, noiseâ€”violations can lead to eviction
Provide Proper Notice: Usually 30-60 days before moving out
Allow Reasonable Access: Let landlord in for repairs/inspections with notice

content.question:
Looking at Emma's situation, what monthly income would she need to comfortably afford her apartment?

What expenses might she have overlooked in her budget?

What would be most important to you in a first apartmentâ€”location, price, size, amenities, or something else?

How would you prioritize these factors?

questions.question: Name three things you should verify before signing a lease agreement.
Why is renter's insurance important, and what does it typically cover that the landlord's insurance doesn't?
Calculate the approximate upfront cost to move into a $1,500/month apartment requiring first/last month's rent plus security deposit.
What are two tenant rights and two tenant responsibilities you should know?

slides.speakerNotes:
ASSESSMENT ANSWER KEY:
1. B (30%)
2. Advantages: Flexibility to move, no maintenance responsibility, lower upfront costs, no property tax. Disadvantages: No equity building, rent can increase, limited control over property, less privacy.
3. $1,100 (security) + $1,100 (first) + $1,100 (last) + $45 (app fee) =$3,345 total upfront
4. "Jointly and severally liable" means each tenant is responsible for the full rent, not just their share. Options: (1) Find a replacement roommate, (2) Negotiate with landlord, (3) Sublet if lease allows, (4) Pay full rent temporarily while pursuing roommate for their share.
5. Look for specific steps like: build savings for deposits, establish credit history, create a rental budget, research typical costs in desired area, maintain good references.

takeaways.title: Read Your Lease Carefully
takeaways.description: Every clause mattersâ€”understand fees, policies, and termination terms before signing

takeaways.title: Budget for True Costs
takeaways.description: Rent is just the beginningâ€”utilities, insurance, and fees add 25-35% to monthly costs

takeaways.title: Plan for Upfront Costs
takeaways.description: Expect to pay 3-4 months of rent before moving in (deposits, fees, first/last)

takeaways.title: Know Your Rights
takeaways.description: Tenants have legal protectionsâ€”learn local laws and document any issues

main.title: íł‹ Rental Property Comparison
main.description: In this activity, you'll research real rental listings and compare them using a structured checklist based on your personal priorities. This helps you practice the research and comparison skills you'll need when looking for your first apartment.

steps: Complete the comparison table for each
Determine which best fits your priorities
